coredata.dc:title: Cysteamine derived N/S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence imaging of pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells
coredata.dc:creator: Pathak, Abhishek
Chandhana, J.P.
Ratheesh, Aswin
Nair, Bipin G.
Suneesh, Punathil Vasu
Satheesh Babu, T.G.
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report cysteamine derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (Cy@CD) for imaging pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells. The Cy@CD was synthesized using one-step simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis with precursors, namely, cysteamine and 50X TAE buffer. The spectroscopic characterization of Cy@CD showed (λex/λem) at 350/434 nm with a decent quantum yield of 13.2%. Cy@CD was deployed in tagging bacterial cells such as E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and B. subtilis and human buccal epithelial cells. The multicolour emissive Cy@CD tagged microbial cells were also cross-validated by fluorometry analysis. The fluorescent Cy@CD could be used as an ideal substitute for the harmful synthetic fluorescent stain in cell imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteamine
50X TAE
Bio-imaging
Pathogenic bacteria
Human buccal epithelial cells
Fluorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multicolor imaging of Cy@CD tagged bacterial cells & hBEC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. We are grateful to ICONN2021-“ Recent Advances in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology”, international conference conducted at SRM University, Chennai (vitrual mode) to provide the platform for presenting our work.

The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. We are grateful to ICONN2021-“ Recent Advances in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology”, international conference conducted at SRM University, Chennai (vitrual mode) to provide the platform for presenting our work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) UV–Vis absorbance (black curve) and PL emission plot (blue curve) of Cy@CD (inset: Cy@CD solution transparent in day light and UV lamp), B) PL emission scan at different excitation wavelength (300–500 nm).
A) HR-TEM micrograph of Cy@CD (Inset: shows the lattice fringes), B) Histogram plot for narrow size distribution of Cy@CD particles, C) XRD analysis & D) FTIR spectra of Cy@CD and Cysteamine.

Fluorescence microscopic images of multicolour emissive Cy@CD tagged bacterial cells viz; A) E. coli, B) S. aureus, C) P. aeruginosa, D) B. subtilis E) hBEC exhibiting blue, green and red colour emission at different excitation wavelength (365, 450 and 525 nm) at 40X magnification.
Pictorial representation of the microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis and purification steps of Cy@CD.

head.ce:title: Cysteamine derived N/S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence imaging of pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita School of Biotechnology, Amritapuri, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 690525, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amrita School of Biotechnology, Amritapuri, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 690525, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report cysteamine derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (Cy@CD) for imaging pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells. The Cy@CD was synthesized using one-step simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis with precursors, namely, cysteamine and 50X TAE buffer. The spectroscopic characterization of Cy@CD showed (λex/λem) at 350/434 nm with a decent quantum yield of 13.2%. Cy@CD was deployed in tagging bacterial cells such as E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and B. subtilis and human buccal epithelial cells. The multicolour emissive Cy@CD tagged microbial cells were also cross-validated by fluorometry analysis. The fluorescent Cy@CD could be used as an ideal substitute for the harmful synthetic fluorescent stain in cell imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Of late, the multifaceted nanoparticles have demonstrated a rising interest in several applications such as drug delivery, photo-ablation therapy and bio-imaging [1]. Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) like metal, silica and quantum dots (QDs) with high surface to volume ratio, tunable photoluminescence emission, and photo-stability have been used for bio-imaging extensively [2,3].

Recently, zero dimensional carbon dots (CDs) emerged as a remarkable subclass of FNPs with strong photoluminescence (PL), resistance to photo-bleaching, biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [4]. The PL emission of CDs can be enhanced by heteroatomic dopingand incorporating surface defects. This results in regulating the electron density over the sp2 hybridised carbon matrix of CDs. The n-type (electron donating heteroatom) or p-type (electron withdrawing heteroatom) doping is achieved by employing heteroatoms such as Nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), Phosphorus (P) and Boron (B) [5]. The nitrogen and sulphur dopant aid in escalating the electron density over carbon dots surface, resulting in enhancement of fluorescence emission [6,7] and have been used immensely in cell imaging application [8]. Moreover, N and S dopants in combination creates multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs and results in modifying the bandgap. This, in turn, modulates the electronic properties of carbon dots with multicolour emission [9]. The nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots have been used in several applications such as heavy metal ion analysis, protein detection and multi-colour cell imaging [10,11,12].

Inspired by our previous work [13], here, we present a novel precursor combination of cysteamine and 50X TAE buffer based nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (Cy@CD) with facile one step microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (as shown in Scheme 1 ). The Cy@CD was screened for cell imaging application and tagged efficiently to bacterial and human buccal epithelial cells (hBEC). To the best of our knowledge, cysteamine based N/S co-doped CD was synthesized for the first time and utilized in a multicolour microbial and mammalian cell imaging application.

Briefly, 10 mg of cysteamine was mixed with 50x TAE buffer, and Cy@CD was synthesized using a domestic microwave at 800 W for 5 mins. The obtained brown coloured charred mass was dissolved in 10 mL autoclaved Milli-Q water. The Cy@CD solution was purified by multi-step purification such as centrifuge, syringe filtration and dialysis (MWCO: 1.0 kDa) and stored at room temperature. The stock concentration of Cy@CD was found to be 1.35 mg/ mL and was diluted to a specific working concentration (2.25 µg/ mL) for further studies.

The characterization of optoelectronic properties of Cy@CD was carried out by UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1 A) shows a typical intense peak at 230 nm and a feeble peak at 350 nm. This attributes to π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 carbon domain (C = C) and n–π* transition due to C = O and nitrogen lone pair of electrons available on the surface Cy@CD [14]. The Cy@CD solution shows transparent pale yellow in day light and bright blue under UV lamp.

A strong PL emission was exhibited at 434 nm corresponding to 350 nm excitation (Fig. 1 B). The intriguing excitation dependent emission property of carbon dots was also observed for Cy@CD during the emission scan at the different excitation wavelength (Fig. 1 B).

As the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 350 nm, the emission intensity increased to the maximum with a redshift. The further change in excitation wavelength from 360 to 500 nm exhibited a dramatic reduction in the PL emission intensity with a perceptible redshift. The excitation dependent emission behaviour of Cy@CD could be due to quantum confinement or the presence of surface defects [15]. The PL emission behaviour was also tested at different ionic concentrations, temperature, UV irradiation time and pH (Fig. S1).

The morphology of the Cy@CD was analyzed by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) to determine its shape and size. Fig. 2 A shows uniformly spherical Cy@CD particles with slender size distribution. The average diameter of fifty Cy@CD particles was calculated as 3.38 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. 2 B). The inset in Fig. 2 A shows the well-resolved lattice fringes with measured d-spacing to be 0.24 nm. This attributes to the graphitic lattice plane (1 0 0) and therefore suggested that Cy@CD possess graphitic structure. The DLS measurement revealed the average hydrodynamic diameter as 5.26 nm, as shown in Fig. S2 A.
XRD analysis of Cy@CD (Fig. 2 C) attributes to the broad diffraction peak and corresponds to the peak of graphitic carbon with 2θ = 20.97°.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of Cy@CD (Fig. 2 D) revealed the functional groups available on the surface of synthesized carbon dots. The sharp peak at 3340 cm−1 ascribed to the presence of primary amine (-NH2). The dual peak at 2940 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of an alkane functional group. The peak at 1630 cm−1 attributes to the unsaturation (C=C). The sharper peak at 1014 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of the organosulphur group (C-S). The presence of the amine and organosulphur group confirms the presence of nitrogen and sulphur dopants on the surface of Cy@CD. To confirm the composition of N1s, S2p, C1s, and O1s elements, the XPS analysis was carried out and shown in Fig. S4. The wide scan spectrum of Cy@CD showed the presence of four typical peaks of C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), O1s (531 eV) and S2p (167 eV). The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C1s displayed two distinguished peaks at 284.8 and 285.8 eV, which represents CC and C-OH/ C-O functional groups. The high resolution spectrum of N1s deconvoluted in three relevant peaks at 398.1, 399.5, 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen, primary amine and pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively. The O1s deconvoluted spectrum exhibited the presence of single peak at 531.1 eV for CO functional group. S2p high resolution spectrum confirms the presence of two peaks at 167.2 and 168.4 eV which confirms the presence of organosulphur group (C-SOx- where x = 2,3,4). The XPS analysis of Cy@CD exhibited the successful doping of nitrogen and sulphur within the matrix of Cy@CD. The results obtained from XPS spectra are in close agreement with the FTIR data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of Cy@CD tagged bacterial cells & hBEC
ce:section.ce:para: The multicolour cell imaging of Cy@CD tagged bacterial cells (E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and B. subtilis) and hBECs were carried out by using a fluorescence microscope. The tagged cells were imaged under different excitation filters such as 365, 450 and 525 nm. The bright field, fluorescence and overlay images of Cy@CD tagged E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and B. subtilis cells were shown in Fig. 3 A & B. The multicolour fluorescence from Cy@CD tagged bacterial cells were evident and can be explained on the basis of non-specific electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Cy@CD (Zeta potential = +10.6 mV, Fig. S2 B) and negatively charged bacterial cells. The fluorescence spectroscopy results also confirm the higher PL response for Cy@CD tagged E. coli than S. aureus cells (Fig. S3 A).

hBEC showed cell internalization of Cy@CD by crossing the cell membrane and tagging the nucleic acid of buccal epithelial cells (Fig. 3 E). The mechanism of cell internalization is based on macropinocytosis. Macropinocytosis is a unique pinocytosis or cell drinking process having large membrane extensions. These extensions resulted into cytoskeleton rearrangement due to which formation of large vesicles takes place. These vesicles trap large number of nanoparticles or carbon dots (Cy@CD). The intake or entrapment of Cy@CD is non-specific and would not be possible in case of phagocytosis, clathrin and caveolae dependent endocytosis pathways. The involvement of macropinocytosis pathways in passing the carbon dots through the cell membrane of adenoid carcinoma cells has been experimentally proven by Wei and co-workers [16]. The Cy@CD tagged hBECs were spotted by overlay merged images of bright field and fluorescence images.

The cytotoxicity level of Cy@CD was also studied using MTT assay on both E. coli and S.aureus cells (Fig. S3 B), and even at the highest concentration of Cy@CD, the cell viability was well above 90% (section S9).

In summary, the bright fluorescent Cy@CD was synthesized by a simple one-step microwave assisted hydrothermal synthesis. The spectroscopic characterization reveals excitation dependent emission properties of carbon dots with a prominent redshift. Cy@CD was established as a potential tagging agent for microbial and mammalian cell imaging with defined mechanistic implications using spectroscopy and zeta potential results. Therefore, the low cost and multicolour Cy@CD makes it a promising candidate for on-site clinical trials with commercial viability.

Abhishek Pathak: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Conceptualization. Chandhana J.P.: Methodology, Visualization. Aswin Ratheesh: . Bipin G. Nair: Funding acquisition, Resources. Suneesh Punathil Vasu: Validation, Formal analysis, Project administration. Satheesh Babu T.G.: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. We are grateful to ICONN2021-“ Recent Advances in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology”, international conference conducted at SRM University, Chennai (vitrual mode) to provide the platform for presenting our work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.130725.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifaceted application of silica nanoparticles. A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Jeelani, P. Mulay, R. Venkat, C. Ramalingam, Multifaceted application of silica nanoparticles. A review. Silicon (2019), 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of nanoparticles commonly used in fluorescent bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.S. Wolfbeis, An overview of nanoparticles commonly used in fluorescent bioimaging, Chem.Soc.Rev. 44(14) (2015) 4743-4768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence Imaging of E. coli using CdSe Quantum Dots, iop conf

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Mohan, A. Pathak, P. Resmi, P. Suneesh, T.S. Babu, Fluorescence Imaging of E. coli using CdSe Quantum Dots, iop conf. Ser. Mater. Sci. Eng, 2019, p. 012107.
B. Yao, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Carbon dots: A small conundrum, TrendsChem. 1(2) (2019) 235-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Xu, T. Kuang, Y. Liu, L. Cai, X. Peng, T.S. Sreeprasad, P. Zhao, Z. Yu, N. Li, Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications, J. Mater. Chem. B 4(45) (2016) 7204-7219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Yang, M. Xu, Y. Liu, F. He, F. Gao, Y. Su, H. Wei, Y. Zhang, Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate, Nanoscale 6(3) (2014) 1890-1895.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent S-doped carbon dots: an emergent architecture for multimodal applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chandra, P. Patra, S.H. Pathan, S. Roy, S. Mitra, A. Layek, R. Bhar, P. Pramanik, A. Goswami, Luminescent S-doped carbon dots: an emergent architecture for multimodal applications, J. Mater. Chem. B 1(18) (2013) 2375-2382.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile pH-sensitive optical detection of pathogenic bacteria and cell imaging using multi-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Pathak, P. Venugopal, B.G. Nair, P.V. Suneesh, T.S. Babu, Facile pH-sensitive optical detection of pathogenic bacteria and cell imaging using multi-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots, Microchem. J. 159 (2020) 105324.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, J.-S. Wei, H.-M. Xiong, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence, Nanoscale 6(22) (2014) 13817-13823.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Pang, H. Gao, S. Wu, X. Li, Facile synthesis the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions, Mater.Lett. 193 (2017) 236-239.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence estimation of total protein in blood serum using N-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Anjali, A. Pathak, P. Suneesh, T.S. Babu. Fluorescence estimation of total protein in blood serum using N-doped carbon dots.Mater. Today: Proc.,33, (2020) 2233-2237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and its application for sensor and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, J. Liu, J. Li, L. Xu, Y. Qiao, One-pot synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and its application for sensor and multicolor cellular imaging, J. Colloid InterfaceSci. 485 (2017) 167-174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor emitting N/S-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for imaging pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Pathak, P. Suneesh, J. Stanley, T.S. Babu, Multicolor emitting N/S-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for imaging pathogenic bacteria and human buccal epithelial cells, Microchim. Acta 186(3) (2019) 157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning of carbon dots emission color for sensing of Fe3+ ion and bioimaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Kailasa, S. Ha, S.H. Baek, S. Kim, K. Kwak, T.J. Park, Tuning of carbon dots emission color for sensing of Fe3+ ion and bioimaging applications, Mater.Sci.Eng. C 98 (2019) 834-842.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor-emitting carbon dots from Malus floribunda and their interaction with Caenorhabditis elegans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T.N.J.I. Edison, S. Perumal, R. Vinodh, Y.R. Lee, Multicolor-emitting carbon dots from Malus floribunda and their interaction with Caenorhabditis elegans, Mater.Lett. 261 (2020) 127153.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The endocytic pathways of carbon dots in human adenoid cystic carcinoma cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wei, X. Jin, T. Kong, W. Zhang, B. Zhu, The endocytic pathways of carbon dots in human adenoid cystic carcinoma cells, Cell Prolif. 52 (2019) e12586.
